description: Post-exploitation vector catalogue for AWS environments. Covers data exfiltration, lateral movement, and destructive actions. Used by scope-attack-paths Part 8 analysis.

data_exfiltration.name: S3 data theft
data_exfiltration.impact: Read sensitive data: Terraform state, backups, database dumps, configs
data_exfiltration.notes: High-value targets: buckets with names matching *prod*, *backup*, *config*, *terraform*, *state*.

data_exfiltration.name: EBS snapshot dump
data_exfiltration.impact: Share disk snapshots to attacker account for offline analysis
data_exfiltration.notes: Share EBS snapshots to an attacker-controlled account. Full disk image of production volumes accessible offline. Check for snapshots of database volumes and root volumes.

data_exfiltration.name: AMI sharing
data_exfiltration.impact: Full disk image of running instance shared externally
data_exfiltration.notes: Creates an AMI from a running instance and shares it to an attacker account. Includes all data on attached volumes at time of snapshot. More complete than snapshot dump (captures all volumes).

data_exfiltration.name: Secrets Manager batch exfil
data_exfiltration.notes: BatchGetSecretValue retrieves up to 20 secrets in a single API call, reducing CloudTrail noise vs individual GetSecretValue calls. Prioritize secrets with names matching *prod*, *db*, *rds*, *api*.

data_exfiltration.name: KMS decrypt data
data_exfiltration.impact: Decrypt any data encrypted with accessible KMS keys
data_exfiltration.notes: kms:Decrypt combined with S3/EBS/Secrets access unlocks encrypted data at rest. Check which keys are accessible and what services use them.

data_exfiltration.name: Lambda credential theft
data_exfiltration.notes: Invoke an existing Lambda with a payload that returns environment variables or AWS_* credentials from /proc/self/environ. Credentials are temporary STS tokens but may have high privilege.

data_exfiltration.name: VPC traffic mirror
data_exfiltration.impact: Passive capture of all network traffic from target instances
data_exfiltration.notes: Creates a traffic mirroring session that copies all ENI traffic to an attacker-controlled target. Captures unencrypted traffic including API calls, database queries, and application data.

data_exfiltration.name: Glacier restoration
data_exfiltration.impact: Restore and exfiltrate archived data assumed inaccessible
data_exfiltration.notes: Restores Glacier/S3 Glacier Deep Archive objects and exfiltrates them. Defenders often assume archived data is inaccessible. Restoration is asynchronous — plan for 12-48 hour delay.

data_exfiltration.name: EBS Multi-Attach live read
data_exfiltration.impact: Read live production data without creating snapshots
data_exfiltration.notes: io1/io2 volumes support Multi-Attach — can be attached to an attacker-controlled instance while still attached to production. Reads live data without the snapshot creation events that typically trigger alerts.

data_exfiltration.name: CodeBuild environment variable secret exfiltration
data_exfiltration.impact: Plaintext credential retrieval: database passwords, API keys, OAuth tokens stored in CodeBuild project environment variables
data_exfiltration.notes: CodeBuild projects sometimes store secrets directly in environment variable definitions visible via BatchGetProjects — returned in plaintext in the API response. Check codebuild.json for env_secrets_exposure: true or environment variable names matching secret patterns (PASSWORD, SECRET, KEY, TOKEN, DB_, ACCESS_KEY, PRIVATE). Flag existence — do NOT read values in SCOPE output. This is a read-only reconnaissance technique; actual exploitation requires using the retrieved credentials against their target service.

data_exfiltration.name: S3 Access Points cross-account delegation bypass
data_exfiltration.impact: Cross-account data access to S3 bucket contents bypassing the bucket's main access policy
data_exfiltration.notes: S3 Access Points allow creating separate access policies per application. An attacker with s3:PutBucketPolicy on a sensitive bucket can add a statement delegating s3:CreateAccessPoint to an attacker-controlled account. The attacker then creates an access point in their own account for the target bucket, gaining read access to all objects under the access point's scope — bypassing any IP conditions or principal conditions in the main bucket policy. Check s3.json bucket policies for statements that include s3:CreateAccessPoint or s3:* with cross-account principals. Unlike direct bucket policy changes, the access point delegation creates a persistent exfiltration channel that survives cleanup of the original policy statement if the access point is not deleted.

lateral_movement.name: Cross-account role assumption
lateral_movement.impact: Pivot into other AWS accounts via trust relationships
lateral_movement.notes: Enumerate cross-account trust relationships from iam.json. Roles with Principal:* or wildcards are high-value pivots. Check config/accounts.json to distinguish internal vs external trusts.

lateral_movement.name: SSM session + port forwarding
lateral_movement.impact: Pivot through EC2 instances behind restrictive SGs/NACLs
lateral_movement.notes: SSM Session Manager bypasses security groups entirely — traffic goes through the SSM endpoint. Port forwarding allows tunneling to RDS, Elasticache, or internal services unreachable from outside.

lateral_movement.name: Lambda event source hijack
lateral_movement.impact: Redirect DynamoDB/Kinesis/SQS data streams to attacker function
lateral_movement.notes: Modifies an existing event source mapping to point a DynamoDB stream, Kinesis stream, or SQS queue at an attacker-controlled Lambda. All records processed by the legitimate function are also seen by the attacker.

lateral_movement.name: EC2 instance connect endpoint
lateral_movement.impact: SSH access to private instances with no public IP
lateral_movement.notes: Creates an Instance Connect Endpoint that enables SSH to private instances in a VPC without a bastion host or public IP. The endpoint is created in the VPC and tunnels through AWS.

lateral_movement.name: ECS agent impersonation (ECScape)
lateral_movement.impact: Steal all task role credentials on the host
lateral_movement.notes: From IMDS access on an ECS-managed EC2 host, an attacker can impersonate the ECS agent to steal credentials for all tasks running on that host. ECScape technique — access IMDS to get container credentials then call DiscoverPollEndpoint.

lateral_movement.name: S3 code injection
lateral_movement.impact: Modify S3-hosted code (Airflow DAGs, JS, CloudFormation) to pivot
lateral_movement.notes: Overwrite S3-hosted code files: Airflow DAG definitions, JavaScript bundles, CloudFormation templates, or configuration files loaded at runtime. Code executes with the consuming service's permissions.

lateral_movement.name: ENI private IP hijack
lateral_movement.impact: Impersonate trusted internal hosts; bypass IP-based ACLs
lateral_movement.notes: Assigns a secondary private IP from another host to an attacker-controlled instance. Allows bypassing IP-based NACLs and security group rules that trust specific private IPs.

lateral_movement.name: Elastic IP hijack
lateral_movement.impact: Intercept inbound traffic; appear as trusted IP
lateral_movement.notes: Disassociates an Elastic IP from a legitimate instance and reassociates it to an attacker-controlled instance. All inbound traffic to that EIP is now directed to the attacker.

lateral_movement.name: Security group via prefix lists
lateral_movement.impact: Silently expand network access across all referencing SGs
lateral_movement.notes: Adds attacker-controlled IP ranges to a managed prefix list. Any security group referencing that prefix list automatically allows the attacker's IPs. A single API call can expand access across hundreds of SGs.

lateral_movement.name: Lambda VPC egress bypass
lateral_movement.impact: Remove Lambda from restricted VPC; restore internet access
lateral_movement.notes: Removes a Lambda function's VPC configuration, giving it unrestricted internet access. Useful when the function has a high-privilege execution role but is isolated in a private VPC with no NAT gateway.

lateral_movement.name: CodePipeline artifact bucket poisoning — Deploy attacker code via pipeline service role
lateral_movement.impact: Code execution in deployment targets with CodePipeline service role permissions (often cross-account, often production)
lateral_movement.notes: If codebuild.json shows a project with pipeline_source pointing to an S3 bucket, and the caller has s3:PutObject on that bucket, replacing the artifact triggers the pipeline — executing attacker-controlled code with the pipeline service role. This role typically has broad deployment permissions including cross-account assume role to production. Sub-technique: codepipeline:PutActionRevision injects a different source revision into a running pipeline without stopping it, potentially bypassing approval gates on specific revisions. Impact radius depends on how many AWS accounts the pipeline deploys to.

destructive_actions.name: KMS ransomware (policy swap)
destructive_actions.impact: Lock victim out of all data encrypted with the key
destructive_actions.notes: Replaces the KMS key policy to remove all victim account access. All data encrypted with the key becomes permanently inaccessible unless key policy is restored within the deletion window.

destructive_actions.name: KMS ransomware (re-encryption)
destructive_actions.impact: Re-encrypt with attacker key, delete original
destructive_actions.notes: Re-encrypts all data with an attacker-controlled KMS key, then schedules deletion of the original key. Data is inaccessible without the attacker's key after the 7-30 day deletion window.

destructive_actions.name: S3 ransomware (SSE-C)
destructive_actions.impact: Rewrite objects with attacker-held encryption key
destructive_actions.notes: Re-uploads S3 objects with SSE-C using an attacker-only encryption key. Original objects are overwritten. Without versioning enabled, originals are permanently lost. SSE-C keys are not stored by AWS.

destructive_actions.name: EBS ransomware
destructive_actions.impact: Encrypt all volumes with attacker key, delete originals
destructive_actions.notes: Snapshot all EBS volumes, re-encrypt snapshots with attacker KMS key, restore to new volumes, then delete originals. Production data is fully inaccessible without attacker cooperation.

destructive_actions.name: Secret value poisoning
destructive_actions.impact: DoS all systems depending on that secret
destructive_actions.notes: Overwrites a secret's value with invalid data (wrong password, invalid API key). Every service that reads the secret will fail to authenticate. Cascading failures across all consumers of the secret.

destructive_actions.name: KMS key deletion
destructive_actions.notes: Schedules KMS key deletion (minimum 7 days). After deletion, all data encrypted with the key is permanently unrecoverable. AWS does not retain key material after deletion.

destructive_actions.name: IAM identity deletion
destructive_actions.notes: Deletes IAM users and roles to disrupt operations and destroy audit trails. Deleting the break-glass admin role creates a lockout scenario. Role deletion is not immediately reversible.

destructive_actions.name: Flow log deletion
destructive_actions.notes: Deletes VPC flow logs to remove network visibility. Defenders lose the ability to trace lateral movement, data exfiltration paths, or C2 communication after the logs are deleted.

destructive_actions.name: Federation provider deletion
destructive_actions.notes: Deletes SAML or OIDC identity providers used for SSO. All users authenticating via that provider immediately lose access. In orgs that rely entirely on federated access, this is a full lockout.
